title: American History
intro: American History questions in this unit draw most heavily on geography: individual states and cities, led by California, Chicago, Texas, Georgia, New York, and San Francisco, anchor a large share of the canon, since tossups and bonuses on state history routinely braid together colonial founding, statehood, and signature later events. Layered onto this geographic backbone is a dense presidential and political roster running from the colonial and Revolutionary founders through every twentieth- and twenty-first-century administration, with Andrew Jackson, Abraham Lincoln, Franklin Roosevelt, and Ronald Reagan among the most frequently asked named individuals. Native American nations and leaders, from the Iroquois Confederacy and Cherokee to the Sioux, Apache, and Nez Perce, recur throughout as both agents and victims of the expansion narrative.

The canon clusters especially densely around a handful of eras: the sectional crisis, Civil War, and Reconstruction generate a large fraction of all questions, spanning battles, generals, constitutional amendments, and the politics of emancipation and its betrayal. The Gilded Age and the conquest of the West supply a similarly rich vein of industrialists, labor conflicts, immigrant groups, and Indian Wars, while the twentieth century contributes a steady stream of material on the Progressive Era, the Depression and New Deal, World War II, and the intertwined stories of the Cold War and the civil rights movement. Supreme Court cases, from Marbury v. Madison to Brown v. Board and Roe v. Wade, and recurring institutions like the NAACP, the Ku Klux Klan, and the Federal Reserve appear across multiple eras rather than in any single one.

More recent decades are represented mainly through presidencies, scandals, and social movements, including Watergate, the Reagan era, the end of the Cold War, and the politics of the 1990s and 2000s, though this material is proportionally thinner than the nineteenth- and mid-twentieth-century core. Colonial topics, by contrast, are a small but steady presence, covering Jamestown, Puritan New England, and recurring conflicts with Native nations that set patterns replayed throughout later expansion.

sections.name: Colonial America
sections.blurb: Settlement, colonial society, and conflict from Jamestown through the eve of the Revolution.

entries.note: Six Nations alliance (Mohawk, Oneida, Onondaga, Cayuga, Seneca, Tuscarora) of upstate New York; largely allied with the British during the Revolution.
First permanent English colony (1607) in Virginia, founded by the Virginia Company; John Smith and Pocahontas figure in its history.
Religious Society of Friends whose pacifism and egalitarianism shaped Pennsylvania under founder William Penn.
Royal governor of the Dominion of New England, overthrown in Boston during the 1689 Glorious Revolution.
Puritan dissenter banished from Massachusetts Bay for antinomian views; helped found Rhode Island settlements.
1676 Virginia uprising led by Nathaniel Bacon against Governor Berkeley over frontier defense policy.
Founder and first governor of the Georgia colony (1732), envisioned as a refuge for debtors.
First governor of Massachusetts Bay Colony; delivered the "City upon a Hill" sermon envisioning a model Puritan society.
1675-76 devastating conflict between New England colonists and a Wampanoag-led Native alliance under Metacomet ("King Philip").
Last Dutch director-general of New Netherland, who surrendered New Amsterdam to the English in 1664.
Puritan minister banished from Massachusetts for advocating religious liberty; founded Rhode Island and Providence Plantations.
Cash crop that saved the Jamestown colony economically, cultivated by John Rolfe and dependent on enslaved and indentured labor.
Quaker proprietor who founded Pennsylvania (1681) as a haven of religious tolerance.
English Caribbean sugar colony whose planter class and slave codes shaped the early Carolina colony.
Puritan minister whose writings fueled the Salem witch trials; also promoted smallpox inoculation.
Quebec city targeted by an unsuccessful 1775 American invasion during the Revolutionary War.
English Protestant dissenters who settled Massachusetts Bay seeking to build a godly "City upon a Hill."
Epidemic disease devastating Native American populations after European contact; inoculation was promoted by Cotton Mather.
Florida city founded by Spain in 1565, the oldest continuously occupied European settlement in the US.
1754-63 North American theater of the Seven Years' War, ending French colonial power and setting up British tax disputes.
Full name of Georgia's founder, who envisioned the colony as a refuge for debtors.
Also called the Delaware; Algonquian nation of the mid-Atlantic who negotiated early treaties with William Penn.
Pre-Columbian Native civilization known for mound-building centers like Cahokia.
Separatist Pilgrim settlement founded 1620 after arriving on the Mayflower; site of the first Thanksgiving.
Powhatan leader's daughter whose relations with Jamestown colonists, including marriage to John Rolfe, eased early tensions.
1754 meeting where Benjamin Franklin proposed his unadopted Plan of Union among the colonies.
New York merchant who seized control during a 1689 uprising against royal authority, later executed for treason.
Christianized Wampanoag interpreter whose murder helped trigger King Philip's War.
Puritan theologian whose fiery sermons like "Sinners in the Hands of an Angry God" drove the First Great Awakening.
Spanish Franciscan friar who founded a chain of California missions in the 1700s.
European power that colonized Florida, the Southwest, and California before ceding territory to the US.
Puritan colony founded 1630 by John Winthrop, centered on Boston.
Ottawa leader of the 1763 uprising against British rule in the Great Lakes region.
Pueblo religious leader who organized the 1680 Pueblo Revolt that drove Spanish colonists from New Mexico.
1739 South Carolina slave uprising, the largest in the colonial South, leading to harsher slave codes.
North Carolina nation defeated in the 1711-15 Tuscarora War, later joining the Iroquois Confederacy as its sixth nation.
Plymouth Colony governor and chronicler of the Pilgrims' journey in Of Plymouth Plantation.
Largest pre-Columbian Mississippian city, near modern St. Louis, known for its earthen mounds.
1730s-40s religious revival led by preachers like Jonathan Edwards and George Whitefield.
Trade commodity driving early colonial economies and exploration, especially French and Dutch ventures.
First representative assembly in English North America, established in Virginia in 1619.
Laborers bound to work a set term in exchange for passage to the colonies, an early source of unfree labor.
Six Nations confederacy of upstate New York, largely allied with the British during the Revolution.
Also called the Delaware; Algonquian nation of the mid-Atlantic who negotiated early treaties with William Penn.
Traditional multi-family dwellings of the Iroquois nations of the Northeast.
Protestant denomination that spread rapidly in America through revivalism and circuit riders.
Easternmost nation of the Iroquois Confederacy, based in what is now upstate New York.
Earthen ceremonial and burial structures built by pre-Columbian Mississippian and earlier Native cultures.
Military commander of Plymouth Colony who helped defend the Pilgrim settlement.
British trade laws restricting colonial commerce to English ships and ports, a source of pre-Revolutionary friction.
Short-lived 1638-55 Swedish colony along the Delaware River, later absorbed by the Dutch and English.
Pennsylvania frontier vigilantes who massacred peaceful Native Americans in 1763 and marched on Philadelphia.
Paramount chief of the Tidewater Virginia confederacy encountered by the Jamestown colonists; father of Pocahontas.
Alliance of Tidewater Virginia nations under Chief Powhatan that first interacted with the Jamestown settlers.
French colonial city and target of failed American invasions in 1775, and a key theater in the earlier French and Indian War.
Cash crop whose cultivation with enslaved labor built the wealth of colonial South Carolina and Georgia.
Spanish colonial territory centered on St. Augustine, ceded to the US in 1819.
Alliance of Northeastern Algonquian nations who resisted English expansion in Maine and the Maritimes.
Longtime royal governor of Virginia whose harsh rule provoked Bacon's Rebellion in 1676.
1715-17 conflict between Southeastern Native nations and English colonists in Carolina, nearly destroying the colony.
French colonial region in Canada's Maritimes whose population was expelled by the British in 1755, some settling in Louisiana as "Cajuns."
Spanish, later Mexican, province encompassing present-day California before US annexation.
Spanish explorer whose shipwreck and years wandering the Gulf Coast produced an early account of the American Southwest.
1636-38 Massachusetts Bay religious dispute centered on Anne Hutchinson's challenge to Puritan orthodoxy.
Notorious pirate (Edward Teach) who terrorized the Atlantic coast before being killed off North Carolina in 1718.
New Mexico site of major pre-Columbian Ancestral Puebloan great houses and ceremonial architecture.
Genoese explorer whose 1492 voyage for Spain initiated sustained European contact with the Americas.
Established church of several colonies, notably Virginia, whose dominance shaped religious dissent and colonization patterns.
First permanent English colony in North America, established at Jamestown in 1607.
Staple crop learned from Native Americans that sustained early colonial settlements.
1677 uprising of settlers against proprietary rule in Albemarle, colonial North Carolina.
Mid-Atlantic waterway Washington famously crossed in 1776 to attack Trenton.
Short-lived 1686-89 consolidated royal colony under Edmund Andros, overthrown after the Glorious Revolution.
British fur-trading enterprise dominating commerce in the Pacific Northwest and Canada.
Six Nations confederacy of upstate New York, largely allied with the British during the Revolution.
British Caribbean sugar colony whose plantation slave society paralleled patterns in the American South.
First permanent English colony (1607), founded by the Virginia Company.
Jamestown colonist who introduced profitable tobacco cultivation and married Pocahontas.
1689 New York uprising led by Jacob Leisler amid the Glorious Revolution's colonial repercussions.
Upper Missouri River nation who hosted Lewis and Clark for the winter of 1804-05.
Puritan woman whose captivity narrative recounted her abduction during King Philip's War.
Surveyed boundary between Pennsylvania and Maryland that later symbolized the divide between free and slave states.
Indigenous peoples of North America whose nations and cultures predated, and were transformed by, European colonization.
One of the Six Nations of the Iroquois Confederacy, notable for aiding the American cause during the Revolution.
1636-38 conflict in which New England colonists nearly destroyed the Pequot nation.
Dutch colonial director who purchased Manhattan Island from Native inhabitants in 1626.
Separatist Pilgrim settlement founded 1620 after arriving on the Mayflower; site of the first Thanksgiving.
1763 Native uprising against British rule in the Great Lakes led by the Ottawa leader Pontiac.
Alternate name for the 1763 Native uprising against British rule led by Pontiac.
Southwestern nations who launched a successful 1680 revolt expelling Spanish colonizers from New Mexico for over a decade.
Fortified French colonial capital targeted by a failed American invasion in 1775.
1702-13 North American theater of the War of the Spanish Succession between English and French colonists.
1760s-70s North Carolina backcountry vigilante movement protesting corrupt colonial officials.
Official colonial name of Rhode Island, founded by Roger Williams for religious dissenters.
Site of the failed 1580s "Lost Colony" English settlement in present-day North Carolina.
Religious institution whose adherents, especially Irish and Italian immigrants, faced discrimination in a largely Protestant nation.
French explorer and "Father of New France" who founded Quebec City in 1608.
Vessels transporting enslaved Africans across the Atlantic to American colonies under brutal conditions.
Colonial gathering places serving as inns, social hubs, and sites of political organizing before the Revolution.
Cash-crop sector that saved the Jamestown colony economically, dependent on enslaved and indentured labor.
New England nation whose leader Massasoit aided the Plymouth Pilgrims; later led by Metacomet in King Philip's War.
Cotton Mather's 1693 book defending the Salem witch trials' legitimacy.
Abundant raw material fueling colonial shipbuilding, construction, and the naval-stores trade.

sections.name: American Revolution and the Founding
sections.blurb: The imperial crisis, the war for independence, and the creation of the constitutional republic.

entries.note: Principal author of the Declaration of Independence; 3rd President; doubled the nation's size with the Louisiana Purchase.
First Treasury Secretary; architect of the national bank and federal assumption of state debts; killed in an 1804 duel with Aaron Burr.
Commander of the Continental Army; presided over the Constitutional Convention; first President.
2nd President and Federalist; signed the Alien and Sedition Acts; father of John Quincy Adams.
Wife of John Adams whose letters urging him to "remember the ladies" reveal Revolutionary-era politics and women's concerns.
Polymath diplomat who secured French support for the Revolution and helped draft the Declaration and Constitution.
First Chief Justice; co-author of the Federalist Papers; negotiated the 1794 Jay Treaty with Britain.
1794 Pennsylvania farmers' uprising against a federal excise tax, suppressed by Washington leading troops personally.
1781 American victory in South Carolina under Daniel Morgan that helped turn the Revolutionary War's Southern campaign.
Green Mountain Boys leader who captured Fort Ticonderoga from the British in 1775.
Constitutional guarantee of speech, press, religion, and assembly, ratified as part of the 1791 Bill of Rights.
Virginia orator famed for "Give me liberty, or give me death!"; later an Anti-Federalist opponent of the Constitution.
American general whose heroics at Saratoga were overshadowed by his 1780 defection to the British as a traitor.
British general whose surrender at Yorktown in 1781 effectively ended the Revolutionary War.
General later exposed as a paid Spanish agent; implicated in the Conway Cabal and Aaron Burr's conspiracy.
Last royal governor of Virginia who issued a 1775 proclamation offering freedom to enslaved people who joined the British.
Undeclared 1798-1800 naval conflict with France under John Adams, sparked by the XYZ Affair.
Last civilian royal governor of Massachusetts, whose loyalist stance and burned mansion inflamed pre-Revolutionary tensions.
Jefferson's first-term VP who killed Alexander Hamilton in an 1804 duel and was later tried for treason over a Western conspiracy.
1775-1783 war in which the thirteen colonies won independence from Britain.
Key ally that provided troops, navy, and funding securing American independence at Yorktown.
Colonists who remained faithful to the British Crown during the Revolution, many later fleeing to Canada.
Silversmith famed for his midnight ride warning of British troop movements before Lexington and Concord.
1797-98 diplomatic scandal in which French agents demanded bribes from US envoys, provoking the Quasi War.
1765-1783 colonial movement for independence from Britain, culminating in war and the founding of the United States.
Weak first US governing framework (1781-89) lacking taxing power, replaced by the Constitution.
Notorious British cavalry officer ("Bloody Tarleton") defeated at the Battle of Cowpens.
1777 American victory in New York that convinced France to enter the war as an ally.
New York fort captured by Ethan Allen's Green Mountain Boys in 1775.
"Swamp Fox" guerrilla leader who harried British forces in the South Carolina backcountry.
"Father of the Constitution"; co-author of the Federalist Papers; 4th President who led the nation through the War of 1812.
Mohawk leader who fought alongside the British against American forces during the Revolution.
New York/Vermont waterway, site of key Revolutionary War and War of 1812 naval battles.
1803 Supreme Court case establishing judicial review under Chief Justice John Marshall.
Enslaved woman at Monticello with whom Thomas Jefferson fathered several children.
Declaration signer and later Supreme Court justice impeached, and acquitted, in 1804 for partisan conduct.
British tax on printed materials that sparked colonial protest under "no taxation without representation."
1767 British taxes on imported goods that provoked colonial boycotts and the Boston Massacre.
Nation founded by the 1776 Declaration of Independence from Britain.
Revolutionary War general who later crushed Native resistance at the Battle of Fallen Timbers (1794).
Long-serving Treasury Secretary under Jefferson and Madison who reduced the national debt.
1777 British victory in Pennsylvania that opened the way to capturing Philadelphia.
1778 New Jersey battle marking the Continental Army's improved discipline after Valley Forge training.
1781 siege where Cornwallis's surrender to Washington and French forces effectively ended the Revolutionary War.
1770 killing of five colonists by British soldiers, used as propaganda for the independence cause.
Alleged 1777-78 plot by officers to replace Washington as Continental Army commander.
French minister ("Citizen Genet") who provoked a diplomatic crisis by recruiting Americans for France against Washington's neutrality.
1774 punitive British laws closing Boston's port after the Tea Party, provoking the First Continental Congress.
Scandal-mongering journalist who first publicized the Jefferson-Hemings relationship allegations.
Central Revolutionary-era rallying concept behind the Sons of Liberty and Patrick Henry's "Give me liberty" rhetoric.
French aristocrat who volunteered for the Continental Army and became a key ally of Washington.
Massachusetts writer whose plays and pamphlets promoted the patriot cause; later wrote an early history of the Revolution.
Massachusetts agitator who organized the Sons of Liberty and helped plan the Boston Tea Party.
Original cabinet post overseeing the Army before being replaced by the Secretary of Defense in 1947.
Colonial resistance organization that protested British taxes through actions like the Boston Tea Party.
1765 British tax on printed materials that sparked colonial protest under "no taxation without representation."
Colonial protests, most famously Boston's in 1773, destroying taxed tea to protest British policy.
Pennsylvania winter encampment (1777-78) where Washington's army endured hardship and drilled under von Steuben.
Massachusetts political dynasty including presidents John and John Quincy Adams and diarist Abigail Adams.
National banner whose stars-and-stripes design dates to the Revolutionary era, traditionally credited to Betsy Ross.
Essays opposing ratification of the Constitution, warning of excessive central power and demanding a Bill of Rights.
1775 costly British victory near Boston that demonstrated colonial militia could stand against regulars.
1780 disastrous American defeat in South Carolina that routed General Gates's forces.
Free Black mathematician and astronomer who helped survey the boundaries of Washington, D.C.
Continental Army general who accepted Cornwallis's sword at the Yorktown surrender.
Spanish governor of Louisiana who aided the American cause by fighting the British along the Gulf Coast.
Polish cavalry officer who fought for American independence and died at the Siege of Savannah.
South Carolina Federalist statesman and diplomat, twice a presidential candidate, remembered for the XYZ Affair retort "millions for defense."
Site, with Lexington, of the first shots of the Revolutionary War in April 1775.
Constitutional provision barring states from impairing contractual obligations, litigated in early Marshall Court cases.
Patriot spy network operating in British-occupied New York during the Revolutionary War.
1776 document, principally authored by Jefferson, proclaiming the colonies' separation from Britain.
James Madison's wife, famed for saving Washington's portrait as the British burned the White House in 1814.
Hamilton-led party favoring strong federal government, the nation's first political party.
New York fort besieged in 1777 and site of an earlier treaty ceding Iroquois land.
New York's first governor and later VP under Jefferson and Madison; opposed ratification of the Constitution.
Frontier militia leader who captured British forts in the Illinois Country during the Revolutionary War.
1796 statement warning against permanent foreign alliances and political factions as Washington left office.
Constitutional Convention delegate credited with drafting the Constitution's final wording, including its Preamble.
Continental Army artillery commander who hauled captured cannon from Ticonderoga to besiege Boston; first Secretary of War.
British general whose army surrendered at Saratoga in 1777, a turning point of the war.
English philosopher whose social-contract theory of natural rights profoundly influenced Jefferson's Declaration of Independence.
Last royal governor of Virginia who issued a 1775 proclamation offering freedom to enslaved people who joined the British.
South Carolina statesman and Constitutional Convention delegate whose Supreme Court Chief Justice nomination was rejected.
Power of courts to strike down unconstitutional laws, established in Marbury v. Madison (1803).
1798 statements by Jefferson and Madison asserting states could nullify unconstitutional federal laws, targeting the Alien and Sedition Acts.
Trade practiced by young George Washington, foundational to establishing colonial property boundaries.
Thomas Jefferson's Virginia plantation home, which he designed himself.
George Washington's Virginia plantation estate on the Potomac.
Region north of the Ohio River organized by the 1787 Northwest Ordinance, which banned slavery there.
British naval force whose impressment of American sailors and blockades provoked conflict in the Revolution and War of 1812.
Cabinet post first held by Thomas Jefferson, overseeing foreign affairs.
1786-87 Massachusetts farmers' uprising over debt that exposed the Articles of Confederation's weakness.
1779 Continental Army campaign that destroyed Iroquois villages allied with the British in New York.
Essays by Hamilton, Madison, and Jay urging ratification of the Constitution.
Early hand-powered submersible used in a failed 1776 attempt to attack a British warship in New York harbor.
Naval branch tracing its origins to the Continental Navy of the Revolutionary War.
US Military Academy on the Hudson River, site of Benedict Arnold's attempted betrayal to the British.
Benjamin Franklin's illegitimate son who remained a loyalist royal governor of New Jersey, estranging the two.
Last royal governor of colonial New York, and earlier North Carolina, who suppressed the Regulator movement.

sections.name: The Early Republic and Jacksonian America
sections.blurb: Party formation, westward expansion, and mass democracy from Washington through the 1850s.

entries.note: 7th President; War of 1812 hero at New Orleans; spoils system, Bank War, and Indian Removal defined "Jacksonian Democracy."
Southeastern nation forcibly removed along the 1838 Trail of Tears to Indian Territory; developed a syllabary under Sequoyah.
Cash crop whose demand, spurred by Eli Whitney's gin, entrenched slavery and drove the antebellum South's economy ("King Cotton").
Conflict with Britain featuring the burning of Washington, the writing of "The Star-Spangled Banner," and Jackson's Battle of New Orleans.
6th President and son of John Adams; negotiated the Adams-Onis Treaty; later fought the gag rule as a congressman.
10th President; first vice president to succeed a president who died in office (Harrison); annexed Texas.
Florida nation that fought three wars against US removal efforts, resisting under leaders like Osceola.
Massachusetts senator and orator famed for his Senate debates with Hayne and Calhoun defending the Union.
8th President and Jackson's VP; built the Democratic Party machine; presided over the Panic of 1837.
1846-48 conflict sparked by the Texas annexation and a border dispute; ended with the Mexican Cession under the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo.
Church founded by Joseph Smith; migrated under Brigham Young to Utah after persecution and Smith's murder at Nauvoo.
Financial crisis following Jackson's Bank War and Specie Circular; caused a severe, prolonged depression under Van Buren.
Party opposing Jackson's executive power, led by Henry Clay and Daniel Webster; collapsed over slavery in the 1850s.
Organization founded 1816 to resettle free Black Americans in Africa, establishing Liberia.
1825 waterway linking the Hudson River to Lake Erie, spurring New York's commercial rise and westward migration.
Commander who won Texas independence at San Jacinto; first president of the Republic of Texas and later US senator/governor.
Shawnee leader who built a pan-Indian confederacy to resist US expansion; died fighting for the British at the Battle of the Thames.
Andrew Jackson's decisive 1815 victory over the British, fought after the Treaty of Ghent had already ended the War of 1812.
"Great Compromiser" senator behind the Missouri Compromise, American System, and Compromise of 1850.
Michigan senator and 1848 Democratic presidential nominee known for advocating "popular sovereignty" on slavery.
Seminole leader who resisted removal from Florida during the Second Seminole War.

entries.topic: Second Bank of the United States
entries.answerline: Second Bank of the United States
entries.note: National bank whose recharter Andrew Jackson vetoed in 1832, sparking the "Bank War."
Early 19th-century Protestant revival movement that fueled reform causes like abolitionism and temperance.
Member of Andrew Jackson's unofficial "Kitchen Cabinet" of advisors.
First American third party, formed in the 1820s opposing Freemasonry and helping pioneer the national nominating convention.
1814 rout of American militia that opened the road for the British burning of Washington.
1836 siege in San Antonio where Texas defenders including Bowie, Crockett, and Travis died fighting Santa Anna's army.
14th President whose support for the Kansas-Nebraska Act deepened sectional strife.
Reformer known as the "father of American public education" for building Massachusetts's common school system.
5th President whose "Era of Good Feelings" produced the Monroe Doctrine opposing European colonization in the Americas.
Long-serving Chief Justice who established judicial review in Marbury v. Madison and strengthened federal power.
Long-serving Supreme Court justice and legal scholar known for his influential Commentaries on the Constitution.
Van Buren's vice president, reputed to have killed Tecumseh at the Battle of the Thames.
Shoshone woman who guided and interpreted for the Lewis and Clark Expedition.
Reform movement against alcohol consumption, later culminating in Prohibition under the 18th Amendment.
"Old Fuss and Feathers" general who won the Mexican-American War's Mexico City campaign; later a Whig presidential nominee.
Early 1800s conflicts with North African states over piracy and tribute, testing the young US Navy.
1836 battle where Sam Houston's Texans defeated and captured Santa Anna, securing independence.
1832 conflict in Illinois/Wisconsin between US forces and Sauk leader Black Hawk's band.
Explorer ("Pathfinder") of the West and the first Republican presidential nominee in 1856.
Founder of the Latter Day Saint movement, murdered by a mob in Illinois in 1844.
West African nation founded by the American Colonization Society as a homeland for freed Black Americans.
1816 Supreme Court case affirming its authority over state court rulings on federal law.
President of the Second Bank of the United States, whose recharter fight with Jackson triggered the Bank War.
Lexicographer whose American Dictionary and spelling books standardized American English.
Ohio college, first to admit women and Black students, a hotbed of abolitionism.
Inventor of the telegraph and Morse code; first message sent in 1844.
"Father of the American Industrial Revolution" who built the first US textile mill from memorized British designs.
Ohio Valley nation led by Tecumseh in resisting American expansion.
Great Basin/Rocky Mountain nation whose member Sacagawea guided Lewis and Clark.
Jackson's 1836 order requiring gold/silver payment for public land, worsening the Panic of 1837.
River transport technology, pioneered by Robert Fulton, that spurred western commerce and the Gibbons v. Ogden case.
Naval hero of the Barbary Wars and War of 1812, famed for burning the captured frigate Philadelphia.
Mexican-American War general and 12th President who died in office in 1850.
Army explorer of the Louisiana Purchase territory, namesake of Pikes Peak.
Mormon leader who succeeded Joseph Smith and led the migration to Utah, founding Salt Lake City.
Political party founded under Andrew Jackson, the nation's oldest continuously operating party.
New York governor who championed and built the Erie Canal.
Jefferson's trade ban aimed at Britain and France that instead devastated American shipping.
1836-44 House rule automatically tabling antislavery petitions, opposed by John Quincy Adams.
19th-century "father of American history," author of a multivolume history of the United States.
1824 Supreme Court case broadly defining federal power over interstate commerce.
Seneca prophet whose early-1800s religious teachings revitalized Iroquois traditions.
"Great Compromiser" senator behind the Missouri Compromise, American System, and Compromise of 1850.
11th President who annexed Texas and Oregon and won the Mexican-American War, fulfilling manifest destiny.
Full name of the 11th President.
Full name of the explorer ("Pathfinder") and first Republican presidential nominee.
1854 law organizing two territories under popular sovereignty, igniting "Bleeding Kansas" violence.
19th-century belief that the US was destined to expand across the continent, justifying territorial acquisitions.
Co-leader with William Clark of the expedition exploring the Louisiana Purchase to the Pacific.
First major peacetime financial crisis in US history, triggered by speculative land bubbles and tightened credit.
Wife of Jackson's Secretary of War whose social ostracism sparked the "Petticoat Affair" cabinet scandal.
Marriage practice of early Mormon settlers that delayed Utah's statehood until the church renounced it.
1842 Supreme Court case upholding federal fugitive slave law over state personal-liberty laws.
Common school system championed by reformers like Horace Mann in the antebellum era.
Independent nation (1836-1845) formed after the Texas Revolution, before US annexation.
West African British colony that, alongside Liberia, became a resettlement destination for freed Black people.
"Little Giant" senator who championed popular sovereignty and debated Lincoln in 1858.
Manufacturing sector pioneered by Samuel Slater and centered on New England mill towns like Lowell.
1835 treaty, signed by a minority Cherokee faction, that ceded tribal land and led to the Trail of Tears.
Maritime industry centered on New England ports like New Bedford and Nantucket, hunting whales for oil.
9th President and War of 1812 general who died just a month into office, the shortest presidential term.
Year of the Compromise of 1850, addressing slavery's status in territory won from Mexico.
1819 treaty in which Spain ceded Florida to the US and defined the western boundary of the Louisiana Purchase.
French observer whose Democracy in America analyzed American society and politics in the Jacksonian era.
Henry Clay's economic program of tariffs, a national bank, and internal improvements to bind the nation together.
Bloodless 1838-39 US-Canada border dispute over Maine's boundary, resolved by the Webster-Ashburton Treaty.
Financial institutions at the center of Jackson's Bank War and recurring 19th-century panics.
Antislavery faction of New York Democrats who split from the party over the Wilmot Proviso.
1813 naval victory where Oliver Hazard Perry declared "We have met the enemy and they are ours."
1846 uprising by American settlers declaring California's independence from Mexico.
Violent 1850s conflict between pro- and anti-slavery settlers in Kansas Territory following the Kansas-Nebraska Act.
1837 diplomatic incident where Canadian loyalists burned an American ship aiding rebels, straining US-British relations.
Revivalist preacher central to the Second Great Awakening, known for emotional "new measures" revivals.
Southeastern nation forcibly removed to Indian Territory along the Trail of Tears in the 1830s.
Constitutional provision giving Congress power over interstate trade, defined broadly in Gibbons v. Ogden.
Southeastern (Muscogee) nation defeated by Andrew Jackson in the Creek War before removal to Indian Territory.
1850s European conflict that indirectly affected US diplomacy and Civil War-era arms sales.
1846-47 westward-migrating group trapped by snow in the Sierra Nevada, resorting to cannibalism to survive.
Reformer who campaigned for humane treatment of the mentally ill, leading to asylum reforms.
Abolitionist newspaper editor killed by a pro-slavery mob in Illinois in 1837.
Period of one-party political calm under James Monroe following the War of 1812.
Lawyer who wrote "The Star-Spangled Banner" after witnessing the 1814 bombardment of Fort McHenry.
Enslaved blacksmith who planned a large 1800 Virginia slave rebellion that was betrayed before it began.
1854 US acquisition of southern Arizona/New Mexico land from Mexico for a southern railroad route.
Influential 19th-century women's magazine shaping fashion and domestic ideals.
1836 execution of surrendered Texan rebels by Santa Anna's forces during the Texas Revolution.
Jacksonian-era policy forcibly relocating Southeastern nations to Indian Territory, exemplified by the Trail of Tears.
South Carolina statesman and VP who championed states' rights, nullification, and slavery's defense.
Full name of the South Carolina statesman who championed nullification and slavery's defense.
1850s nativist political party opposing Catholic and immigrant influence in American politics.
Slave ship whose captives' 1839 revolt led to a Supreme Court case, argued by John Quincy Adams, freeing them.
South Carolina "War Hawk" congressman and later president of the Second Bank of the United States.
Massachusetts mill town, a pioneering center of factory textile production employing young women.
Pioneering textile mill town employing young women in early industrial factories.
1819 Supreme Court case upholding the national bank and federal supremacy over states.
Institutions whose reform, championed by Dorothea Dix, sought more humane treatment of the mentally ill.
Capital captured by US forces in 1847, ending the Mexican-American War.
Second president of the Republic of Texas, known for aggressive expansionism and Native removal policies.
Mormon settlement built by Joseph Smith, site of his 1844 murder by a mob.
1832-33 standoff after South Carolina declared federal tariffs void, resolved when Jackson threatened force and Congress compromised.
Utopian religious commune in New York practicing "complex marriage," founded by John Humphrey Noyes.
Overland wagon route from Missouri to the Pacific Northwest used by tens of thousands of settlers.
Nation founded by the 1804 slave revolt whose independence influenced US slavery debates.
1817 agreement demilitarizing the Great Lakes between the US and Britain after the War of 1812.
Russian colonial territory in present-day Alaska, sold to the US in 1867.
Commander who won Texas independence at San Jacinto; first president of the Republic of Texas.
Cherokee silversmith who created a syllabary enabling his nation's written language.
National museum and research complex founded 1846 from a British bequest by James Smithson.
"Father of Texas" who led American colonization of the region under Mexican authority, laying groundwork for the Texas Revolution.
Law enforcement/paramilitary force established during the Texas Republic era, active against Native peoples and Mexican forces.
Longtime Missouri senator ("Old Bullion") who championed hard currency, westward expansion, and Jacksonian Democracy for thirty years.
1835-36 bloodless border dispute between Ohio and the Michigan Territory over the Toledo Strip.
1842 agreement settling the Maine-Canada border dispute between the US and Britain.
Maritime industry centered on New England ports like New Bedford and Nantucket, hunting whales for oil.
Naval commander during the Barbary Wars and War of 1812, captain of the USS Constitution.
Failed 1846 proposal to ban slavery in territory won from Mexico, intensifying sectional tensions.

sections.name: Civil War and Reconstruction
sections.blurb: Sectional conflict over slavery, the war between North and South, and its unfinished aftermath.

entries.note: Union general who accepted Lee's surrender at Appomattox; 18th President whose Reconstruction-era administration was scandal-plagued.
16th President who preserved the Union, issued the Emancipation Proclamation, and was assassinated in 1865.
Escaped slave who became a leading abolitionist orator, publisher, and advisor to Lincoln.
Turning-point 1863 battle in Pennsylvania featuring Pickett's Charge; site of Lincoln's Gettysburg Address.
White-supremacist terrorist organization founded after the Civil War to intimidate freedpeople; resurged nationally in the 1920s.
1863 Confederate victory in Georgia; the war's bloodiest two-day battle in the Western theater.
Surname of the reform-minded family including preacher Henry Ward Beecher and novelist Harriet Beecher Stowe.
Massachusetts abolitionist senator caned by Preston Brooks in 1856 after his "Crime Against Kansas" speech.
Radical abolitionist who led the Pottawatomie Massacre and the 1859 raid on the Harpers Ferry armory.
Formerly enslaved abolitionist and women's rights speaker famed for her "Ain't I a Woman?" address.
Union general defeated at Fredericksburg; his distinctive facial hair gave rise to the word "sideburns."
1861-1865 conflict between the Union and the seceded Confederate States over slavery and states' rights.
Post-Civil War period (1865-1877) of rebuilding the South and extending rights to freedpeople, ended by the Compromise of 1877.
Capital of the Confederacy, captured by Union forces in April 1865.
Lincoln's Secretary of State who arranged the Alaska Purchase ("Seward's Folly"); survived an assassination attempt the night Lincoln was shot.
17th President who succeeded Lincoln; clashed with Radical Republicans over Reconstruction and was impeached, acquitted by one vote.
15th President whose inaction before secession is often blamed for worsening the sectional crisis.
Surname of Robert E. Lee, commanding general of the Confederate army who surrendered at Appomattox.
Lincoln's 1863 executive order declaring enslaved people in Confederate territory free.
First major land battle of the Civil War (1861), a surprising Confederate victory near Manassas, Virginia.
Secretary of State under Grant who negotiated the Treaty of Washington settling Civil War-era claims against Britain.
Escaped slave who led dozens to freedom on the Underground Railroad and served as a Union scout and spy.
Prominent abolitionist preacher, brother of Harriet Beecher Stowe, later embroiled in a notorious adultery scandal.
Capital of the Confederacy, captured by Union forces in April 1865.
Chief Justice who wrote the 1857 Dred Scott decision denying Black Americans citizenship.
Bloodiest single day of the Civil War (1862) in Maryland; gave Lincoln the opening to issue the Emancipation Proclamation.
Bloody 1862 battle in Tennessee where Grant's Union army repelled a surprise Confederate attack.
1864 Union attempt to breach Confederate lines at Petersburg by tunnel-mine explosion, ending in disaster.
German-American reformer, Union general, and senator who championed civil service reform.
Ohio "Copperhead" Democrat arrested and exiled for antiwar dissent during the Civil War.
Surname of Jefferson Davis, president of the Confederate States of America.
Reconstruction-era amendment granting citizenship and equal protection, later the basis for many civil-rights rulings.
Formerly enslaved people whose new legal freedom and citizenship were contested during Reconstruction.
Federal agency aiding formerly enslaved people with education, labor contracts, and relief after the Civil War.
Author of Uncle Tom's Cabin, whose depiction of slavery galvanized Northern abolitionist sentiment.
President of the Confederate States of America throughout the Civil War.
Abraham Lincoln's wife, whose life was marked by the deaths of children and her husband's assassination beside her.
Union general who headed the Freedmen's Bureau and founded Howard University.
Confederate general-in-chief who surrendered the Army of Northern Virginia at Appomattox in 1865.
Nine-month 1864-65 Union siege of a Virginia rail hub that finally broke Lee's army.
Radical Republican congressman who championed harsh Reconstruction terms and Black civil rights, leading Andrew Johnson's impeachment.
Transcendentalist minister and abolitionist whose sermons inspired Lincoln's "of the people, by the people" phrasing.
Full name of the Union general and 18th President.
1862 Union victory in Tennessee where Grant demanded "unconditional surrender," earning his nickname.
1864 disastrous Confederate assault in Tennessee that decimated the Army of Tennessee's officer corps.
1862 lopsided Confederate victory in Virginia where Union troops made futile assaults on entrenched heights.
Kentucky abolitionist and Lincoln's minister to Russia, known for his fiery antislavery advocacy.
Breakaway slaveholding republic of eleven seceded Southern states, led by Jefferson Davis (1861-65).
Northern Democrats who opposed the Civil War and favored a negotiated peace with the Confederacy.
Never-ratified 1861 proposed constitutional amendment that would have protected slavery to avert secession.
Confederate commerce raider built in Britain that destroyed Union shipping, prompting the postwar "Alabama Claims" arbitration.
Union Civil War veterans' fraternal organization, influential in Gilded Age Republican politics and pensions.
Surname of Sarah and Angelina Grimke, South Carolina sisters who became prominent abolitionist and women's rights advocates.
Legal right to challenge detention, controversially suspended by Lincoln during the Civil War.
Financier who marketed Civil War bonds; his firm's 1873 failure triggered a nationwide panic.
Post-war ideology romanticizing the Confederacy and downplaying slavery's role in causing the Civil War.
Confederate cavalry general blamed for the Fort Pillow massacre; later the first Grand Wizard of the Ku Klux Klan.
Chief Justice who wrote the 1857 Dred Scott decision denying Black Americans citizenship.
Lincoln's Treasury Secretary and later Chief Justice, who presided over Andrew Johnson's impeachment trial.
Derogatory term for white Southerners who supported Reconstruction-era Republican governments.
Lincoln's first Secretary of War, removed amid corruption allegations over war contracts.
Mississippi city whose 1863 siege and surrender gave the Union control of the Mississippi River.
Radical abolitionist publisher of The Liberator who demanded immediate emancipation.
Union general famed for his destructive "March to the Sea" through Georgia in 1864.
British parliamentarian and abolitionist whose campaign against the slave trade influenced American antislavery movements.
Civil War militia units, both Union and Confederate, wearing flamboyant French-inspired North African uniforms.
Decade encompassing secession, the Civil War, and the start of Reconstruction.
Decade of Reconstruction's final years, ending with the Compromise of 1877 and withdrawal of federal troops.
Union strategy devised by Winfield Scott to blockade Southern ports and split the Confederacy along the Mississippi.
Notorious Confederate POW camp in Georgia where thousands of Union prisoners died of disease and starvation.
1862 battle ending the Seven Days Battles, where massed Union artillery repelled Confederate assaults.
1862 battle in Kentucky that ended Braxton Bragg's Confederate invasion of the state.
1864 brutal Overland Campaign battle featuring intense fighting at the "Bloody Angle."
Family of actors including John Wilkes Booth, who assassinated Lincoln in 1865.
Confederate general who commanded the Army of Tennessee, notably at Chickamauga and Perryville.
Derogatory term for Northerners who moved South during Reconstruction, often to participate in Republican politics.
Civil War battlefield nurse who founded the American Red Cross in 1881.
1873 mass killing of Black Louisiana militiamen by white paramilitaries, a landmark Reconstruction-era atrocity.
Site of Harriet Tubman's 1863 Combahee River Raid freeing hundreds of enslaved people in South Carolina.
Nickname for the American South, popularized as a Confederate anthem during the Civil War.
Early-20th-century historiographical school portraying Reconstruction as a corrupt failure, since discredited.
New York City mayor who proposed the city secede and trade with the Confederacy for cotton profits.
Surname of Hamilton Fish, Grant's Secretary of State.
Compromise of 1850 provision requiring the return of escaped slaves, intensifying Northern abolitionist backlash.
Enslaved people who escaped bondage, often via the Underground Railroad, becoming a central legal and political flashpoint.
Union general nicknamed "the Rock of Chickamauga" for his stand covering a Union retreat.
Lincoln's Secretary of the Navy who built the Union blockade fleet.
Virginia site of John Brown's 1859 armory raid intended to spark a slave uprising.
First Black member of the US Senate, elected from Mississippi during Reconstruction.
Former Confederate guerrilla who became a notorious outlaw and bank/train robber after the war.
Constitutional Union Party's 1860 presidential candidate, seeking to avoid the sectional crisis over slavery.
Union colonel who defended Little Round Top at Gettysburg, earning the Medal of Honor.
Abbreviation for the Ku Klux Klan, the white-supremacist terrorist organization founded after the Civil War.
Secret pro-Confederate society active in the North during the Civil War, associated with Copperhead sympathizers.
Confederate diarist whose detailed wartime journal is a major primary source on Southern elite life.
Union cavalry general known for his Shenandoah Valley campaign and later Indian Wars command.
Post-Civil War period (1865-1877) of rebuilding the South and extending rights to freedpeople.
Abraham Lincoln's eldest son, later Secretary of War and a successful railroad executive.
Full name of Lincoln's Treasury Secretary and later Chief Justice.
1863 Union siege that gave control of the Mississippi River after the city's surrender.
Institution of enslaved labor whose expansion and restriction drove sectional conflict culminating in the Civil War.
Enslaved people whose labor and status were central to the antebellum South's economy and the war that ended slavery.
Secret network of routes and safe houses helping enslaved people escape to freedom, aided by figures like Harriet Tubman.
1861 diplomatic crisis after the Union seized Confederate envoys from a British ship, nearly drawing Britain into the war.
1863 Union operation under Grant that captured the Mississippi River stronghold, splitting the Confederacy.
Lincoln's Secretary of State who arranged the Alaska Purchase; survived an assassination attempt the night Lincoln was shot.
Grant's Secretary of War impeached for accepting bribes in a trading-post scandal.

sections.name: The Gilded Age and the West
sections.blurb: Industrialization, immigration, labor conflict, and the conquest of the trans-Mississippi West.

entries.note: New York Tribune editor famed for "Go West, young man"; 1872 Liberal Republican challenger to Grant.
Large 19th-century immigrant group who settled the Midwest as farmers and brewers; faced suspicion during World War I.
Spanish colony whose 1898 liberation led to the Platt Amendment; later scene of the Bay of Pigs and Missile Crisis under Castro.
"Great Commoner" who gave the "Cross of Gold" speech and ran for president three times on free silver and populism; later prosecuted the Scopes trial.
Immigrant laborers who built the transcontinental railroad; faced exclusion under the 1882 Chinese Exclusion Act.
Plantation cash crop grown by enslaved and immigrant labor in Louisiana, Cuba, and Hawaii; central to sugar-trust and tariff politics.
Corrupt Democratic political machine dominating New York City politics, associated with boss William "Boss" Tweed.
25th President who led the US through the Spanish-American War and annexation of the Philippines; assassinated in 1901.
23rd President (1889-93), grandson of William Henry Harrison; presided over the Sherman Antitrust Act and rapid Western state admissions.
Plains nation involved in the Sand Creek Massacre and the Battle of the Little Bighorn.
Immigrant group who fled the Famine to fill Northeastern cities and factories; came to dominate urban machines like Tammany Hall.
Mining-boom metal (Comstock Lode) fueling Western settlement; center of the 1890s "free silver" monetary debate.
Dominant Southern Plains horse nation that resisted white settlement in Texas until surrendering at Fort Sill in 1875.
"Continental Liar from the State of Maine"; Republican Secretary of State and 1884 presidential nominee defeated by Cleveland.
1905 Supreme Court case striking down a maximum-hours law, epitomizing the era's "liberty of contract" jurisprudence.
1898 war sparked partly by the sinking of the USS Maine; won the US Cuba, Puerto Rico, Guam, and the Philippines.
Newspaper magnate whose "yellow journalism" helped push the US toward the Spanish-American War.
Railroad and shipping magnate ("Commodore") who built one of the era's great industrial fortunes.
Cavalry officer annihilated with his command by Lakota and Cheyenne forces at the Little Bighorn (1876).
Automaker who pioneered the moving assembly line and $5 workday, making cars affordable to the masses.
1896 Supreme Court decision upholding "separate but equal" racial segregation.
Transcontinental and trunk lines whose construction and monopolistic practices drove industrialization and Populist backlash.
Pacific archipelago ceded by Spain in 1898; scene of a bloody US insurrection before eventual independence in 1946.
First woman to run for US president (1872); suffragist and stockbroker who championed free love.
Southwestern nation whose resistance, led by figures like Geronimo and Cochise, continued into the 1880s.
Agrarian class whose debt and rate grievances fueled the Grange and Populist movements of the late 1800s.
Immigrant group arriving after US annexation of the Philippines, working largely in West Coast agriculture.
Automaker founded by Henry Ford, pioneer of the moving assembly line and $5 workday.
Apache leader who led the last major Native resistance campaign before surrendering in 1886.
Precious metal whose 1848 California discovery and later Western strikes drove mining rushes and monetary politics.
Only president to serve two non-consecutive terms (22nd and 24th); vetoed pensions and battled the Panic of 1893.
Violent 1892 clash between Carnegie Steel workers and Pinkerton guards at Andrew Carnegie's Pennsylvania plant.
Full name of the Republican "Continental Liar from the State of Maine," the 1884 presidential nominee.
Banker who consolidated industries like steel (US Steel) and personally stabilized markets during the Panic of 1907.
Fiery labor organizer who campaigned against child labor and for coal miners' unions.
Investigative journalist famed for feigning insanity to expose asylum abuses and for circling the globe in under 80 days.
Mass-circulation press whose "yellow journalism," led by Pulitzer and Hearst, shaped public opinion toward war with Spain.
Agrarian third party of the 1890s advocating free silver and railroad regulation, epitomized by William Jennings Bryan.
New York "Stalwart" Republican senator and patronage boss who clashed with President Garfield.
1864 attack by Colorado militia that slaughtered a peaceful Cheyenne and Arapaho encampment.
Industry dominated by Andrew Carnegie's mills and later US Steel, symbolizing industrial-era capitalism.
Communication technology pioneered by Samuel Morse that transformed news, business, and rail coordination.
Site of the 1890 massacre of Lakota Ghost Dancers by US troops, and later a 1973 AIM occupation.
1890 killing of Lakota Ghost Dance followers by the US Army in South Dakota, ending the Indian Wars.
Anti-vice crusader whose namesake laws banned mailing obscene material and birth-control information.
1876 battle where Custer's cavalry was annihilated by Lakota and Cheyenne forces.
Educator who founded Tuskegee Institute and urged Black economic self-help via his "Atlanta Compromise" address.
Mining commodity driving boomtowns like Butte, Montana, amid the era's Western mineral rushes.
Industry centered on Western towns like Butte, Montana, fueling late-19th-century mineral booms.
California labor agitator whose anti-Chinese rhetoric helped drive passage of the Chinese Exclusion Act.
New York harbor immigration station processing millions of arrivals from the 1890s onward.
Caribbean nation whose 1804 slave-revolt independence influenced US slavery debates; later occupied by US Marines (1915-34).
Commodity harvested and shipped commercially before refrigeration, an important 19th-century industry.
Journalist and activist who led anti-lynching crusades and co-founded the NAACP.
Immigrant group arriving in large numbers after 1880, settling Northeastern cities and facing discrimination like the 1891 New Orleans lynchings.
Lincoln's secretary turned Secretary of State who authored the Open Door Policy toward China.
1889 Pennsylvania dam-failure disaster that killed over 2,000 people.
Southwestern nation whose "Long Walk" removal in the 1860s and later Code Talkers in WWII mark its history.
Plains nation whose 1920s Oklahoma oil wealth led to the murders investigated in "Killers of the Flower Moon."
Infrastructure whose "Good Roads Movement" and later the interstate system tied the nation together.
1885 killing of Chinese miners by white workers in Wyoming Territory amid anti-Chinese labor tensions.
Industrial commodity whose vulcanization and later Akron-centered manufacture (Goodyear, Firestone) fueled the auto age.
Cabinet post overseeing federal lands, Native affairs, and natural resources.
Lakota holy man and leader during the Battle of the Little Bighorn and the later Ghost Dance movement.
Republican faction under Roscoe Conkling defending patronage politics against civil-service reform.
Rockefeller's oil monopoly, broken up by the Supreme Court in 1911 under antitrust law.
Plantation crop underlying Louisiana's and, after annexation, Hawaii's and Cuba's economies.
Women's suffrage leader who co-founded the National Woman Suffrage Association with Elizabeth Cady Stanton.
Major newspaper of record that later published the Pentagon Papers in 1971.
Prolific inventor of the phonograph and practical incandescent light bulb, founder of a research lab in Menlo Park.
Widespread disease ("consumption") whose sanatorium treatment shaped public-health policy into the 20th century.
Engineer who built the Los Angeles Aqueduct, enabling the city's growth amid California water disputes.
American "filibuster" who briefly seized control of Nicaragua in the 1850s.
Inventor credited with patenting the first practical telephone in 1876.
Steel magnate who built US Steel and became a major philanthropist funding libraries.
Early major American railroad whose 1877 wage cuts sparked the nationwide Great Railroad Strike.
1876 battle where Custer's cavalry was annihilated by Lakota and Cheyenne forces.
Late-19th-century transportation craze that promoted women's mobility and dress reform.
Full name of the educator who founded Tuskegee Institute and urged Black economic self-help.
Corrupt leader of New York's Tammany Hall machine, exposed by cartoonist Thomas Nast.
Pro-business, small-government faction of the post-Reconstruction Democratic Party.
Montana mining city whose massive copper deposits fueled Gilded Age industrial fortunes.
Ranching industry driving Texas cattle drives along trails like the Chisholm Trail to railheads.
Disappointed office-seeker who assassinated President Garfield in 1881, spurring civil-service reform.
Ethnic enclaves formed by Chinese immigrants in Western cities amid exclusion-era discrimination.
1882 law banning Chinese immigration, the first major US law restricting immigration by nationality.
Manufactured good produced heavily by Cuban and immigrant labor in cities like Tampa and Key West.
Atlanta-invented soft drink (1886) that became a symbol of American consumer capitalism.
Chiricahua Apache leader who resisted US expansion into Arizona before making peace in 1872.
1894 march of unemployed workers on Washington led by Jacob Coxey demanding public-works relief.
Wyoming trading post and treaty site central to US-Plains Indian relations.
Carnegie's steel partner whose harsh handling of the Homestead strike made him a target of an assassination attempt.
Full name of the 20th President, assassinated by Charles Guiteau in 1881.
Full name of Lincoln's secretary turned Secretary of State who authored the Open Door Policy.
Ohio senator and Treasury Secretary, namesake of the Sherman Antitrust Act.
Newspaper publisher whose "yellow journalism" rivalry with Hearst helped push the US toward war with Spain.
Extralegal mob killings, mostly of Black Southerners, prevalent from the 1880s through the mid-20th century.
Recorded-music business born from Edison's phonograph and Tin Pan Alley song publishing in the late 19th century.
Powerful Rhode Island senator and financial reformer whose plan shaped the Federal Reserve System.
Petroleum industry built by Rockefeller's Standard Oil into one of the era's dominant monopolies.
Petroleum sector centered on Rockefeller's Standard Oil monopoly, later broken up by antitrust action.
Financial crisis triggered by railroad overexpansion and Jay Cooke's bank failure, causing a long depression.
Private security firm hired by industrialists to break strikes, notably at Homestead in 1892.
Short-lived 1860-61 horseback mail relay service across the West, quickly made obsolete by the telegraph.
Transcontinental and trunk-line network whose construction and monopolistic rates drove industrialization and Populist backlash.
Volunteer cavalry regiment led by Theodore Roosevelt that fought in the Spanish-American War.
Democratic presidential candidate who won the popular vote in 1876 but lost the disputed election to Hayes.
Mass-manufactured household technology, pioneered by Isaac Singer, that transformed clothing production.
Rockefeller's oil monopoly, broken up by the Supreme Court in 1911 under antitrust law.
Political cartoonist whose drawings exposed Boss Tweed and popularized the modern images of Uncle Sam and Santa Claus.
Historically Black college founded by Booker T. Washington in Alabama in 1881.
Site of the 1898 coup and massacre overthrowing a biracial city government, entrenching Jim Crow rule.
Women's reform organization founded 1874 advocating alcohol prohibition and other moral reforms.
Popular motivational lecture by Russell Conwell preaching that opportunity and wealth lay close to home.
Sport that developed from rugby in late-19th-century college contests, notably at Ivy League schools.
San Francisco Bay immigration station, the "Ellis Island of the West," that processed mostly Asian immigrants under exclusionary scrutiny.
Legal framework, beginning with the 1890 Sherman Act, aimed at breaking up monopolies like Standard Oil.
Wealthy dynasty founded by fur trader John Jacob Astor, later prominent in New York real estate and society.
Fencing invention that ended the open range and transformed Western ranching and land disputes.
National pastime professionalized in the late 1800s, later integrated by Jackie Robinson in 1947.
1887 document forced on Hawaii's King Kalakaua at gunpoint, stripping the monarchy of power in favor of American and European businessmen.
Plains animal nearly hunted to extinction in the 19th century, undermining Native American subsistence.
South Dakota mountains sacred to the Lakota, whose 1874 gold discovery triggered treaty violations and war.
Landmark suspension bridge completed 1883 connecting Manhattan and Brooklyn, engineered by the Roebling family.
Illustrator whose "Gibson Girl" defined a popular ideal of American femininity around 1900.
Full name of the disappointed office-seeker who assassinated President Garfield in 1881.
Adult education and cultural movement offering traveling lectures and entertainment to rural communities.
21st President who succeeded the assassinated Garfield and surprised critics by championing civil service reform.
Suquamish and Duwamish leader for whom the city of Seattle is named, known for a speech on Native land loss.
Major post-Civil War cattle-driving route from Texas to Kansas railheads.
Industrial workforce whose organizing and strikes, as at Homestead and Ludlow, shaped labor conflict.
Substance once a common ingredient in patent medicines before regulation under the Pure Food and Drug Act.
Legal concept central to "liberty of contract" jurisprudence that struck down labor regulations like in Lochner.
Lakota war leader who helped defeat Custer at the Little Bighorn.
Cabinet agency overseeing federal lands, national parks, and Native American affairs.
Retail innovation such as Macy's and Wanamaker's, transforming urban shopping in the late 19th century.
Women's rights leader who organized the 1848 Seneca Falls Convention and co-founded the National Woman Suffrage Association.
Milled grain commodity whose production, centered in Minneapolis mills, powered a major industrial sector.
Army officer who captured Filipino insurgent leader Aguinaldo, ending a key phase of the Philippine-American War.
Monetary policy movement advocating unlimited silver coinage, championed by William Jennings Bryan.
Army general who campaigned against the Apache and Sioux during the Indian Wars.
Cavalry officer annihilated with his command at the Battle of the Little Bighorn (1876).
Late-19th-century Native religious revival prophesying restoration, ended violently at Wounded Knee.
Mineral strikes, from California to the Klondike, that drove waves of Western migration and boomtowns.
Monetary system pegging currency to gold, defended by conservatives against the free-silver movement.
Pacific island ceded by Spain after the 1898 Spanish-American War, becoming a US territory.
1886 Chicago labor rally bombing that led to the execution of anarchist organizers and a backlash against labor radicalism.
Author of A Century of Dishonor and Ramona, novels exposing the mistreatment of Native Americans.
Source of a major wave of 19th-century immigration, especially following the Great Famine.
20th President, assassinated by disappointed office-seeker Charles Guiteau in 1881.
Immigrant religious group arriving in large numbers from Eastern Europe, settling urban centers and facing discrimination.
State and local statutes enforcing racial segregation across the South from the 1880s through the 1960s.
Naturalist and conservationist who co-founded the Sierra Club and championed national parks like Yosemite.
Explorer who led the first expedition through the Grand Canyon and later directed the US Geological Survey.
1896-99 stampede of prospectors to the Canadian Yukon, launching Seattle as a supply hub.
Disease whose sufferers, especially in Hawaii, were forcibly isolated at colonies like Molokai.
Author (Samuel Clemens) whose satirical writing gave the Gilded Age its name.
Chicago dynasty founded on Cyrus McCormick's reaper invention and later the Chicago Tribune.
Reform-minded Republicans who bolted the party in 1884 to support Cleveland over corruption concerns.
Federally designated lands where Native nations were confined following removal and the Indian Wars.
Southwestern nation whose "Long Walk" removal in the 1860s and later Code Talkers in WWII mark its history.
Pacific Northwest nation whose 1877 flight toward Canada under Chief Joseph ended in surrender just short of the border.

entries.topic: Noble and Holy Order of the Knights of Labor
entries.answerline: Noble and Holy Order of the Knights of Labor
entries.note: Broad-based 1880s labor union that declined after being blamed for the Haymarket bombing.
Severe economic depression triggered by railroad overexpansion and bank failures, fueling Populist politics.
Financial crisis averted partly by J.P. Morgan's personal intervention, spurring creation of the Federal Reserve.
Once-abundant bird driven to extinction by overhunting and habitat loss by the early 20th century.
Intellectual-property grants that protected inventions like Edison's and Bell's, fueling the era's industrial innovation.
Immigrant group arriving in large numbers from the 1880s, settling industrial cities like Chicago and Detroit.
Vice targeted by Progressive-era reformers concerned with urban "white slavery" and moral reform.
1894 nationwide rail strike led by Eugene Debs, crushed by federal troops under Cleveland.
Pacific archipelago ceded by Spain in 1898; scene of a bloody US insurrection before independence in 1946.
Surname of John D. Rockefeller, founder of the Standard Oil monopoly.
19th President whose disputed 1876 election was resolved by the Compromise of 1877, ending Reconstruction.
Full name of the 19th President.
1890 law banning monopolistic business combinations, the first major federal antitrust legislation.
1898 explosion of a US battleship in Havana harbor that helped spark the Spanish-American War.
Plains nation (Lakota/Dakota/Nakota) central to conflicts including the Little Bighorn and Wounded Knee.
1866 undersea cable enabling near-instant communication between North America and Europe.
Rail lines, completed with the 1869 golden spike, linking the East and West coasts and spurring industrial growth.
1868 agreement recognizing Lakota control of the Black Hills, later violated after gold was discovered there.
Historically Black college founded by Booker T. Washington in Alabama in 1881.
Disputed election between Hayes and Tilden resolved by the Compromise of 1877, ending Reconstruction.
1893 Chicago world's fair showcasing American industrial and cultural achievement, featuring the first Ferris wheel.
Mosquito-borne disease whose repeated outbreaks, notably in the South, shaped public health efforts and Panama Canal construction.

sections.name: The Progressive Era and World War I
sections.blurb: Reform movements, trust-busting, women's suffrage, and America's entry into the First World War.

entries.note: 28th President who led the US through World War I with his Fourteen Points and pushed for the League of Nations.
Civil rights organization founded 1909 that fought segregation through litigation, including Brown v. Board.
26th President; trust-busting Progressive reformer who led the Rough Riders and built the Panama Canal.
27th President and later Chief Justice; his split from Theodore Roosevelt's progressivism prompted the 1912 Bull Moose campaign.
Target of the US Open Door Policy (1899); later a WWII ally and, under Nixon, a Cold War diplomatic opening.
Radical labor union ("Wobblies") advocating "one big union" and direct action, founded 1905.
Abbreviation for the Industrial Workers of the World, the radical "Wobblies" labor union.
First Jewish Supreme Court justice; pioneered the "Brandeis brief" and championed a constitutional "right to privacy."
Long-serving Supreme Court justice known for free-speech opinions like Schenck v. United States ("clear and present danger").
1914-18 global war the US entered in 1917 under Wilson, ending with the Treaty of Versailles.
Labor leader who founded the Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters and pressured FDR to desegregate defense industries.
1927 Supreme Court decision upholding forced sterilization, infamous for Holmes's line "three generations of imbeciles are enough."
Anarchist writer and activist deported during the Red Scare for her radical politics.
"Bull Moose" party formed by Theodore Roosevelt in 1912 to challenge Taft on reform issues.
Muckraking author of The Jungle, whose expose of meatpacking spurred the Pure Food and Drug Act.
Republican Chief Justice and 1916 presidential nominee who narrowly lost to Wilson.
Reform target of Progressive-era activists, leading to laws restricting factory work by minors.
Defense attorney famed for the Scopes trial and the Leopold and Loeb case.
Pseudo-scientific movement advocating selective breeding, leading to forced-sterilization laws upheld in Buck v. Bell.
1914 killing of striking coal miners' families by Colorado militia during a labor dispute.
Italian anarchist whose followers were blamed for a wave of early-20th-century bombings, including the Wall Street bombing.
Birth-control activist who founded the organization that became Planned Parenthood.
Chicago-centered industry whose unsanitary conditions, exposed in Sinclair's The Jungle, spurred food-safety regulation.
Scholar-activist and NAACP co-founder who championed the "Talented Tenth" and challenged Booker T. Washington's accommodationism.
Movement culminating in the 1920 Nineteenth Amendment granting women the vote.
1920 constitutional amendment granting women the right to vote.
Labor leader who founded the Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters and pressed FDR to desegregate defense industries.
Military draft policy implemented in WWI and WWII, later a flashpoint of Vietnam-era protest.
Secretary of War and State under Roosevelt/Taft who reorganized the Army and won the Nobel Peace Prize.
First US Forest Service chief and close ally of Theodore Roosevelt's conservation policy.
Massachusetts senator who led opposition blocking US entry into the League of Nations.
Progressive California governor and Bull Moose VP nominee; later an isolationist senator.
Alternate name for the IWW, the radical "Wobblies" labor union.
Social reformer who founded Hull House in Chicago, pioneering the settlement house movement.
American journalist who chronicled the Russian Revolution in Ten Days That Shook the World.
Jamaican-born Black nationalist who founded the UNIA and promoted Pan-Africanism and Black economic self-reliance.
Attorney General and Secretary of State known for "Dollar Diplomacy" under Taft.
1906 law regulating food and drug labeling, passed after Upton Sinclair's expose of the meatpacking industry.

entries.topic: Pure Food and Drug Act of 1906
entries.answerline: Pure Food and Drug Act of 1906
entries.note: Law regulating food and drug labeling and safety, spurred by The Jungle's meatpacking revelations.
1919 Supreme Court case upholding WWI-era speech restrictions under the "clear and present danger" test.
NAACP magazine founded and edited by W.E.B. Du Bois to advocate for Black civil rights.
26th President; trust-busting Progressive reformer who led the Rough Riders and built the Panama Canal.
1911 New York factory fire killing 146 workers, spurring major workplace safety reforms.

Woodrow Wilson's second wife who effectively managed presidential affairs after his 1919 stroke.
Socialist Party leader and five-time presidential candidate, imprisoned for opposing WWI conscription.
Pseudo-scientific reform movement advocating selective breeding, leading to forced-sterilization laws.
1913 law creating the Federal Reserve System as the nation's central bank under Wilson.
Central banking system created by the 1913 Federal Reserve Act to stabilize currency and credit.
